speaker: interviewer
text: Just one second. This is tape five with survivor Michael Steinstock. OK, you were telling us about coming to this country for a visit and what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back after two months and every papers what I have, diplomas, I keep for my cousins, first cousins, my uncle's children. And the Embassy, after two months, sent me a letter to come over to the Embassy, American Embassy.

speaker: interviewer
text: I come there. Make me X-rays and looked over my health. And said to eight, 10 days you get answer if you can go to America to not. After 10-- eight days, I get a letter, you're OK. You have to come with your passport, Polish passport. And I went for the passport. [INAUDIBLE] go, no. I educated you here. You cost a lot of money. You want to go to America? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is take three years. In 1966, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I get the-- the passport, and I went to the American Embassy and said, wait a minute, you have to go over again everything. Maybe in three years, you're sick. A lot of doctors, a lot of X-rays, everything is OK. I get the visa, and I can take my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my son, this time by mine-- I have a picture here-- from my sister's husband's brother's son. But remember the two girls I have? And my boy, take care of my boy. Now, my boy is this time 12 years, a real big boy. And I come over to this country in 1966, April the first. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SOBS] I come into the country here, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I start working. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My cousin find me a factory. I never work in a factory. And I start working, and I get the first check. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] First check, I get $40. And I come home, I tell Sylvia, to my cousin-- I lived by him. I'm so happy. Well, why you so happy for? [LAUGHS] I said, Sylvia, the President from Poland don't have $40, you know? I-- then somebody get me that job here in Castor Avenue, in Mirrow's. Which is a fur store, we should mention.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the work, and I worked there 29 years, in that one place, like a furrier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you were a tailor?

speaker: interviewee
text: A furrier. A tailor and a furrier.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a furrier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived in what-- just-- just describe the neighborhood that you lived in.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in this neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is Northeast Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Northeast Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remain close to other survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, I belong to the-- the New American Club, the survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son has grown up since he was 12 in this country.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in 1970, my son comes over to this country. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In 1971, I get American citizen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my son is not-- is not 18 years. He get automatical.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that make you very happy? Yes. You are now in a second marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zofia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and I know that you are a very proud grandfather, so why don't we mention--

speaker: interviewee
text: This girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son is with a wife 12 years. She lost her baby from six months. She lost a baby before. And this is OK. She's a doll. I call her my queen, the young queen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this will be my last question to you. You've been through an enormous amount in your life, a great deal of trouble. As we've said, there's no way to sum it up in two hours. But if you had to give a message to your beautiful granddaughter, Eden-- If you wanted to sort of give her your thoughts about what your life has meant, could you tell me just how you feel looking back on all these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, maybe a philosophy, or a feeling about what you've been through and what it's meant to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: What it's like? What's going to happen to me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me? Just any thoughts about how the war and the Holocaust affected you, and what you hope for her.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do I hope? I hope for my granddaughter, if I have [INAUDIBLE], peace. No different from what nation. I don't like when I hear somebody's killed children, the women, the people. I don't like this. I'm very-- you know, I'd like peace for everybody in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a wonderful, wonderful legacy for your granddaughter. Thank you very much. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We're in Philadelphia. We're in the beautiful living room of my Michael Steinstock, and we've just completed an interview for Shoah. And now, Michael, perhaps you could introduce us to the lady by your side. OK? Would you introduce us to your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Zofia Steinstock. And I'm married ten years. And a very nice-- she's very nice, and love her. She's a good cook, and she's a and a nice personality. And she's beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, she is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the best thing that she cooks for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everything, OK, OK. And the only other question I want to ask you now is, you mentioned earlier that you wanted a chance to say how you feel about living in this country.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very happy. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I'm lucky I'm in this free country like United States of America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we're very lucky and happy that you joined us here. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture's taken in Poland, in Lublin, in 1935. My sister, Sara, and me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's all. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The picture's taken in Poland in the city of [? Rostov, ?] in the New Year, and my son is, at this time, four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken-- 1954, he's born. The picture's taken 1958, for a party and for my place where I work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zenon, Z-E-N-O-N. In Jewish, it's [? Selik. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can talk? The picture's taken in [? Rostov, ?] Poland in 1956. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And who is it of?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is it? Who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me, Michael Steinstock. You can recognize it. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] The picture's taken in Philadelphia. This is my son, my daughter-in-law, her mother. This is their wedding picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are-- what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name for my daughter-in-law is Phyllis Steinstock.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and when was the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wedding is in 1989.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter-in-law, Phyllis, with the granddaughter, Eden. And-- How old is Eden in that picture? In the picture's, Eden two years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewer
text: At your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my cousin's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my granddaughter Eden at my house. And she is, here, two years old. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, you'll definitely mention--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my older cousin, Sylvia Venador The picture taken in her house in Philadelphia. And she brings me to America. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] She and her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isidor Venador.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my granddaughter, Eden. She's three-year-old in the picture and made in Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think she's one of the most beautiful women you've ever seen?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is She's a little queen. Beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: We agree.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love her.
